neighborhoods.name: French Quarter / Vieux Carré
primary_groups: French Creoles, Spanish colonists, free people of color, later Italian and Irish immigrants
neighborhoods.character: The original colonial city, laid out in a grid along the Mississippi River's natural levee. By the 19th century, the Quarter was the commercial and cultural heart of Creole New Orleans — its narrow streets lined with iron-balconied townhouses, courtyard gardens hidden behind carriage gates. After the Civil War, the Quarter declined as Creole families moved uptown, and working-class Sicilian immigrants filled the old buildings. The smell of coffee roasting at the river wharves, horse dung, and subtropical rot defined the sensory landscape.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} arrived in New Orleans before the Civil War, the Vieux Carré was the city — everything began at the levee, where steamboats and ocean vessels unloaded passengers into a city that looked and sounded more like Marseilles or Havana than anything in the American interior.

neighborhoods.name: Tremé
primary_groups: Free people of color (gens de couleur libres), African Americans, Haitian refugees, Creoles of color
neighborhoods.character: The oldest Black neighborhood in the United States, adjacent to the French Quarter. Founded by free people of color who purchased land outside the colonial city walls. Tremé was home to a sophisticated Creole-of-color community — property owners, artisans, musicians, and professionals who occupied a middle tier in New Orleans' three-caste racial system. Congo Square, at the neighborhood's edge, was the only place in antebellum America where enslaved people could legally gather to play music and dance on Sundays — the birthplace of jazz traditions.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Tremé was unlike anywhere else in America — if {ancestor_name} was a free person of color, this neighborhood offered something no other city could: a community of Black property owners, artisans, and professionals who had been free for generations before the Civil War.

neighborhoods.name: Irish Channel
primary_groups: Irish famine immigrants, later German immigrants
neighborhoods.character: A narrow strip of riverside blocks in the Lower Garden District, bounded by the river, Magazine Street, and the Garden District's mansions. Irish immigrants settled here in the 1830s-40s, drawn by work on the docks and the New Basin Canal (which killed thousands of Irish laborers from yellow fever and malaria). The neighborhood was rough, low-lying, and subject to flooding. Frame shotgun houses packed the muddy streets. St. Alphonsus and St. Mary's Assumption churches anchored the Irish and German communities on adjacent blocks.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was Irish and landed in New Orleans, the Channel was likely home — a muddy, fever-ridden strip where Irish dock workers lived in shotgun houses and St. Alphonsus Church held the community together through yellow fever seasons that killed by the thousands.

neighborhoods.name: Little Palermo / French Quarter-adjacent
primary_groups: Sicilian immigrants, primarily from Palermo province and surrounding villages
neighborhoods.character: Sicilian immigrants settled in and around the lower French Quarter and along Decatur Street near the French Market. They dominated the produce trade, truck farming in the surrounding parishes, and the fishing industry at the lake. The community was tight-knit and insular — Sicilian dialect was the daily language, and mutual aid societies organized by village of origin provided insurance and social life. The 1891 lynching of eleven Sicilians following the murder of police chief David Hennessy was the largest mass lynching in American history and traumatized the community for generations.
key_institutions: Contessa Entellina Society (mutual aid by village)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Sicilian, the French Quarter's lower end was Little Palermo — the produce stalls at the French Market were Sicilian-run, and mutual aid societies organized by the exact village you came from provided the safety net in a city that had lynched eleven Italians in 1891.

neighborhoods.name: Back-of-Town / Central City
primary_groups: African Americans, formerly enslaved people, later Great Migration arrivals
neighborhoods.character: The neighborhood stretching back from the river in the uptown area, low-lying and frequently flooded. After emancipation, formerly enslaved people settled in the cheapest available housing — the back-of-town areas furthest from the natural levee and most prone to standing water and mosquitoes. Shotgun houses lined unpaved streets. Despite crushing poverty, the neighborhood produced a disproportionate share of New Orleans' musical talent — jazz, blues, and later rhythm and blues emerged from these blocks.
key_institutions: Central City churches (numerous Baptist and AME congregations)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Back-of-Town was where Black New Orleans lived when there was nowhere else to go — if {ancestor_name} settled in Central City after emancipation, the shotgun house on an unpaved street was likely the starting point, and the neighborhood's churches and second-line traditions were the sustaining institutions.

neighborhoods.name: Garden District / Faubourg St. Mary (American Sector)
primary_groups: Anglo-American merchants, planters, and professionals from the upper South and Northeast
neighborhoods.character: When Americans flooded into New Orleans after the Louisiana Purchase, the established Creole families rejected them. The Americans built their own city uptown from Canal Street — the 'neutral ground' dividing line. The Garden District became home to the wealthiest Americans: cotton factors, sugar brokers, and commission merchants who built Greek Revival and Italianate mansions surrounded by live oaks and iron fences. The wealth was built on slave labor and the cotton trade.
key_institutions: Toby's Corner (oldest house in the district, 1838)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was an Anglo-American merchant arriving after the Louisiana Purchase, the Garden District was the aspiration — the mansions on Prytania and St. Charles were built with cotton and sugar money, and Canal Street was the dividing line between the Creole old city and the American new one.

major_employers.name: Port of New Orleans — Stevedoring and Longshoremen
major_employers.wages: Cotton screwmen (the most skilled) earned $4-6/day (1880s-1900s) — among the highest wages for manual labor. General longshoremen earned $1.50-3/day. Work was irregular, controlled by gang bosses and shaped by racial divisions.
major_employers.working_conditions: The port was the city's economic engine. Work was brutal — cotton bales weighed 500+ pounds and were compressed ('screwed') into ship holds by hand. The labor force was divided by race in a unique pattern: the Longshoremen's Protective Union Association (Black) and the Cotton Men's Executive Council (white) alternated gangs on the docks in a fragile biracial arrangement. The 1907 general levee strike united Black and white workers briefly. Italian immigrants were brought in as strikebreakers, fueling ethnic tension.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The docks were where New Orleans earned its living — if {ancestor_name} worked the levee, {pronoun_subject} was part of the most physically demanding labor in the city, screwing cotton bales into ship holds in subtropical heat alongside men of every race and nationality.

major_employers.name: Sugar Refineries
major_employers.wages: $1-2/day for laborers (1870s-1890s). Work was seasonal, peaking during the grinding season from October to January.
major_employers.working_conditions: Louisiana sugar plantations fed raw sugar to New Orleans refineries along the river. The work was hot, dangerous — open kettles of boiling sugar caused severe burns. Before the Civil War, enslaved workers ran plantation sugar houses. After emancipation, freedpeople and immigrants did the same work for wages. The American Sugar Refining Company (Domino Sugar) consolidated the industry by the 1890s.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Sugar built New Orleans before cotton did — if {ancestor_name} worked the refineries along the river, the grinding season from October to January was the most grueling and best-paid stretch of the year.

major_employers.name: Illinois Central Railroad / Southern Pacific Railroad
major_employers.wages: Section hands earned $1-1.50/day (1890s-1910s). Skilled shop workers earned $2-4/day. Pullman porters earned $27.50/month (1920s) plus tips.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Illinois Central connected New Orleans to Chicago — it was the Great Migration's iron highway. The railroad's terminal at Canal Street and the Southern Pacific's terminal were major employment centers. Railroad work was divided by race: Black workers were confined to porter, fireman, and section hand jobs. White workers held engineer and conductor positions. The Brotherhood of Railroad Trainmen excluded Black workers until 1960.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The Illinois Central ran from Canal Street to Chicago — if {ancestor_name} worked the railroad, {pronoun_subject} knew the route that carried thousands of Black Southerners north during the Great Migration, and the train itself was both employer and escape route.

major_employers.name: United Fruit Company
major_employers.wages: Dock workers earned standard longshoremen rates. Office and warehouse positions paid $15-25/week (1920s-1930s).
major_employers.working_conditions: United Fruit made New Orleans the banana capital of the world — millions of bunches arrived at the company's wharves annually. The company's headquarters at 321 St. Charles Avenue was one of the most powerful corporate addresses in the hemisphere. Dock workers unloaded bananas by hand in sweltering holds. United Fruit's political influence in Central America and the Caribbean was immense and troubling.
major_employers.narrative_hook: United Fruit turned New Orleans into the banana capital of America — if {ancestor_name} worked the company's wharves, {pronoun_subject} unloaded fruit from the same ships that carried American corporate power into Honduras and Guatemala.

port_of_entry_details.facility: New Orleans was a major international port of entry. Immigrants arrived directly by ocean vessel at the Mississippi River wharves. Before the Civil War, New Orleans was the second-busiest immigration port after New York. European immigrants — especially German and Irish — arrived on ships that had carried cotton to Europe and returned with human cargo.
port_of_entry_details.processing: New Orleans had no facility equivalent to Ellis Island until the federal government opened a processing station at the foot of Julia Street in 1849. The Louisiana Immigration Station later operated at the river. Passenger manifests were filed with the Collector of Customs. Many records were damaged or lost during the Civil War occupation.
records_generated: Ship passenger manifests (New Orleans Customs records, available at NARA and on Ancestry)
Orleans Parish civil and sacramental records (among the richest in the US due to French/Spanish record-keeping traditions)
Notarial archives (unique to Louisiana — notaries recorded property transfers, marriage contracts, slave sales, and emancipation documents)

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: New arrivals rented rooms in boarding houses near the docks or in their ethnic enclave. The shotgun house — narrow, one-room-wide, with rooms in a line — was the dominant housing form for working-class New Orleanians of every race. Wealthier arrivals took rooms in the American sector boarding houses above Canal Street.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: New Orleans' settlement patterns reflected the three-caste system. Creoles of color occupied Tremé and the Seventh Ward. Anglo-Americans built uptown from Canal Street. Irish settled the Channel. Sicilians claimed the lower Quarter. Black communities formed in back-of-town areas on the lowest ground. The pattern was more complex and permeable than in Northern cities — racial and ethnic mixing was more common, especially before 1900.
housing_patterns.trajectory: The trajectory varied dramatically by race and caste. Free people of color owned property from the colonial era. White immigrants followed a boarding house to shotgun house to camelback house progression. Formerly enslaved people faced the most restricted trajectory — confined to back-of-town shotgun houses with few paths to ownership. The city's below-sea-level geography meant that the poorest residents lived on the lowest, wettest, most disease-prone land.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: New Orleans' racial violence operated within a uniquely complex three-caste system. The post-Civil War collapse of the free-people-of-color middle caste into a binary racial order was devastating. Anti-immigrant violence targeted Italians and Irish in distinct episodes.
specific_incidents: 1891 mass lynching of eleven Sicilian men at Parish Prison — the largest mass lynching in American history, following the murder of police chief David Hennessy
1866 New Orleans Massacre — police and white mobs attacked a Black suffrage convention at the Mechanics' Institute, killing 34
1874 Battle of Liberty Place — White League militia overthrew the Reconstruction government in a coup
1900 Robert Charles Riot — white mobs rampaged through Black neighborhoods for four days after a Black man resisted arrest
nativist_organizations: White League (1874-1876, paramilitary overthrow of Reconstruction)
White Citizens' Council (1950s-60s, organized resistance to desegregation)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Augustine Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Free people of color, Creoles of color, later African Americans broadly
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers from 1842 — invaluable because they record free people of color with racial designations. Sacramental records are in French, Spanish, and Latin. Held by Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: St. Augustine in Tremé was the parish of New Orleans' free people of color — if {ancestor_name} was a free person of color before the Civil War, the church's sacramental registers are among the most important genealogical sources in the country, recording race, status, and family connections in meticulous detail.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Alphonsus Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Irish immigrant community in the Irish Channel
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers from 1855. Parish records in English. Held by Archdiocese of New Orleans.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: St. Alphonsus was the Irish Channel's anchor — if the {surname} family was Irish and lived in New Orleans, the church's registers probably documented their sacraments, and the Redemptorist fathers who ran it kept careful records.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Société d'Économie et d'Assistance Mutuelle
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Free men of color, later Creole community broadly
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership records, financial records, and meeting minutes. Economy Hall's records are held by the Amistad Research Center at Tulane University — one of the most important collections documenting free Black life in antebellum America.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Société d'Économie was the most prestigious mutual aid society for free men of color — if {ancestor_name} was part of New Orleans' Creole-of-color elite, the Economy Hall membership rolls may document the family's standing in a community that had no parallel elsewhere in America.

source_references.author: Walter Johnson
source_references.title: Soul by Soul: Life Inside the Antebellum Slave Market
source_references.notes: Groundbreaking study of the New Orleans slave market — the largest in the domestic slave trade — documenting the experience of enslaved people sold in the Faubourg Marigny markets and the economics of human commodification.

source_references.author: Lawrence N. Powell
source_references.title: The Accidental City: Improvising New Orleans
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history of New Orleans from founding through the antebellum period, covering the French, Spanish, and American colonial periods, immigration, and the development of the city's unique racial and cultural order.

source_references.author: Ned Sublette
source_references.title: The World That Made New Orleans: From Spanish Silver to Congo Square
source_references.notes: Cultural history tracing New Orleans' musical and racial traditions from colonial origins through Congo Square, documenting the African, Caribbean, French, and Spanish influences that created Creole culture.

source_references.author: Richard Campanella
source_references.title: Geographies of New Orleans: Urban Fabrics Before the Storm
source_references.notes: Detailed geographical and demographic analysis of New Orleans' neighborhoods, documenting how topography, drainage, and race shaped settlement patterns from the colonial era through the 20th century.
